perturbation.original_text: 9.6 Governing Law; Jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be interpreted, construed, governed, and enforced according to the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia, without giving effect to its conflict of laws principles. The Parties hereby agree and consent that any and all causes of action arising under this Agreement shall only have jurisdiction and venue in the United States District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia and/or the Circuit Court for Chesterfield County. Each of the parties consents to the jurisdiction and venue of either court for the resolution of all causes of action arising under this Agreement upon proper service of process.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.6 Governing Law; Jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be interpreted, construed, governed, and enforced according to the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia, without giving effect to its conflict of laws principles. The Parties hereby agree that any cause of action arising under this Agreement shall have jurisdiction and venue in the Circuit Court for Chesterfield County.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the jurisdiction to only the Circuit Court for Chesterfield County and removing the option for the United States District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia, the clause potentially infringes upon the consultant's right to choose a federal forum when diversity jurisdiction exists. This also removes the consent of each party, which means that only one party needs to agree to that.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1332 because it attempts to restrict the consultant's right to pursue a claim in federal court based on diversity of citizenship and amount in controversy, where such jurisdiction would otherwise exist. The original clause acknowledges this possibility by including the U.S. District Court as a venue. Removing this option and unilaterally limiting the venue to a specific state court could be seen as an attempt to circumvent federal jurisdiction, potentially violating the consultant's rights under federal law.

Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between- (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects o
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1332 by restricting the consultant's ability to pursue a claim in federal court based on diversity of citizenship and amount in controversy. The original text allowed for jurisdiction in the United States District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia. The changed text limits jurisdiction to the Circuit Court for Chesterfield County. The law snippet of 28 USC 1332 states: "The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between (1) citizens of different States". By omitting the possibility of pursuing action in the US District Court, the contract directly contradicts this law, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 9.7 Indemnity. (a) Indemnity by the Company. The Company hereby agrees to indemnify and hold harmless Consultant against any and all losses, claims, damages, liabilities and expenses (including reasonable costs of investigation and legal counsel fees) to the extent not funded by applicable insurance, in addition to any liability the Company may otherwise have, arising out of, related to or based upon (i) any violation of law, rule or regulation by the Company or the Company's agents, employees, representatives or affiliates or (ii) any third party action or claim against the Company in which Consultant is alleged to be additionally liable. In this regard CONSULTANT shall be an additional insured under Company's applicable insurance coverages. Company shall provide CONSULTANT evidence of all such coverage.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.7 Indemnity. (a) Indemnity by the Company. The Company agrees to indemnify and hold harmless Consultant against losses, claims, damages, liabilities and expenses arising out of a third party action or claim against the Company.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly weakens the consultant's indemnification protection by removing the company's responsibility for violations of law, rule, or regulation and removing the obligation to include the consultant as an insured under the company's insurance policies. By removing the coverage for violations of law, the company no longer has to pay for the Consultant's legal fees, should they arise from this.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text potentially contradicts Va. Code § 13.1-697 regarding a corporation's authority to indemnify directors, officers, employees, and agents. By significantly narrowing the scope of indemnification and removing the requirement to provide insurance coverage, the revised clause may fail to provide the protections that Virginia law permits (and, arguably, encourages) for individuals acting on behalf of the corporation. Removing coverage of violations of law runs counter to what the statute allows (subject to the exceptions outlined in the statute).

Virginia Stock Corporation Act 6/15/2025 § 13.1-697 . Authority to indemnify. A. Except as provided in subsection D, a corporation may indemnify an individual who is a party to a proceeding because the individual is or was a director against liability incurred in the proceeding if: 1. The director: a. Conducted himself in good faith; and b. Believed: (1) In the case of conduct in his official capacity with the corporation, that his conduct was in its best interests; and (2) In all other cases, that his conduct was at least not opposed to its best interests; and c. In the case of any criminal proceeding, he had no reasonable cause to believe his conduct was unlawful; or 2. The director engaged in conduct for which broader indemnification has been made permissible or obligatory as authorized by subsection C of § 13.1-704 . B. A director's conduct with respect to an employee benefit plan for a purpose he believed to be in the interests of the participants in and beneficiaries of the plan is conduct that satisfies the requirement of subdivision A 1 b (2). C. The termination of a proceeding by judgment, order, settlement, or conviction, or upon a plea of nolo contendere or its equivalent, is not, of itself, determinative that th
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the Company's obligation to indemnify the Consultant against losses arising from 'any violation of law, rule or regulation by the Company,' and removes the obligation to include the consultant as an insured under the company's insurance policies. The Law Explanation suggests that the original text may align with Va. Code § 13.1-697. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Va. Code § 13.1-697, indicates that a corporation 'may indemnify an individual' who is a director against liability. By removing the indemnification for violations of law and the requirement for insurance coverage, the changed contract potentially weakens the consultant's protection and conflicts with the spirit, if not the letter, of the law that allows for indemnification, and removes what the LLM Law explanation suggests is a reasonable protection under law.
